story:
The alarm yanks me out of a winter-dark morning. My studio is cold; my feet are ice. I reach for my device, hush the sound, and let the flex-screen rise. Habit takes over. Before coffee, before thoughts, there’s the app.

Outlast.

The green screen, the trumpet fanfare, the same message: Congratulations! You are still in the game. It’s a game that isn’t a game—my microchip streaming vitals and location to the cloud, the app tallying how many people born in 2004 have already died. Twelve years of normalizing surveillance means I barely think about it now. I only watch the bar creep, savor the dread and the relief.

Today’s number loads. 133,926,129 people born in my year. I’ve outlasted 30,581,020. It ticks upward—death in real time. It always makes me grimy, and still, I feel that guilty flicker: I’m winning.

I shuffle to make coffee and oatmeal, stingy with heat and money, wrapped in a hoodie. I check again. Trumpets. The counter spins and stops: 42,007,831. My breath seizes. More than ten million in minutes. The count surges. This isn’t thousands a day. It’s millions a minute.

I split the screen, scan the news. No wars breaking, no quakes, no ash clouds, no aliens. Just scattered reports of outages, dropped calls, and emergency networks jamming under some sudden, nameless strain. I reload. 47,852,203. Faster. Too fast. My palms slick. I call Hailey. She hates talking about Outlast, most of us do—it’s a private vice with a sour aftertaste—but she picks up.

I beg her to check. She does. Her screen blasts the same brass, then: You have outlasted… 54,030,901. Her face drains. We switch her birth year to 2020. The counter creeps by one. Normal.

“Glitch,” she insists, trying to sound steady. She wants to go to work. Then someone starts pounding at her door hard enough to rattle the frame. I shout through the connection: don’t open it, not with our cohort’s toll spiking like this. She says I’m spiraling, that we’ll get a beer later, and hangs up.

The apartment hums with cold. Coffee spreads across the floor in a brown fan. I watch the numbers climb and wonder if one of them is Hailey. The thought makes my stomach drop. I rock in my chair, tell myself I wanted to win, but not like this—not as monarch of a massacre. I want to die old, loved, ordinary.

A plan flares: leave town, bus west, disappear into scrub and canyons. I move fast. Clothes, granola bars, cash from the soup can. I zip the bag. The count blazes past seventy million. My heart claws, but motion steadies me. I pocket the device, go to the door.

My screen vibrates in my hand. Proximity alert. I freeze, hand on the knob. The app’s brass still seems to echo behind my eyes. Everyone born in 2004 is vanishing, but not 2020. No global catastrophe. No disaster alerts. Just a targeted scythe cutting through my year. Surveillance hums in my veins. Someone—or something—knows where I am, how I’m doing, whether I’m still breathing. It doesn’t need to knock. It already found me.

I hold my breath. The room shrinks, the cold sharpens. My hand tightens on the handle. I listen to the device buzz in the hush, for trumpets, for anything that might tell me if I’m still in the game.
